content.body: Linear programming finds the maximum or minimum of an objective function subject to linear constraints. It is widely used in business and engineering.

Steps:
1. Define variables (let x = ..., y = ...)
2. Write objective function: Maximise/Minimise Z = ax + by
3. Write constraints as inequalities
4. Draw feasible region
5. Test corner points of feasible region

content.title: LP Example
content.body:
Maximise Z = 3x + 2y subject to:
x + y ≤ 4,  x ≥ 0,  y ≥ 0

Corner points: (0,0), (4,0), (0,4)
Z at (0,0) = 0
Z at (4,0) = 12
Z at (0,4) = 8
Maximum Z = 12 at (4,0)

questions.question: In linear programming, the optimal solution always occurs at:
options: A corner point of the feasible region
questions.answer: A corner point of the feasible region
questions.explanation: The simplex theorem guarantees the optimal is at a vertex (corner point)

questions.question: The feasible region contains all points that:
questions.explanation: The feasible region is the set of all points satisfying every constraint

questions.question: Maximise Z = x+y subject to x≤3, y≤3, x,y≥0. Maximum Z is:
Which type of problem can linear programming NOT solve?
questions.explanation: Linear programming handles linear (straight-line) relationships only

questions.question: The objective function in LP is the function you want to:
questions.explanation: The objective function is what you're maximising or minimising
